coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive colorimetric biosensor for the detection of Gram-positive bacteria by integrating paper-based enrichment and carbon dot-based selective recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Li
Deng, Suqi
Luo, Yimin
Fu, Qiang
Fan, Yi
Jia, Li
coredata.dc:description: Rapid screening of bacteria by low-cost and eco-friendly material-based approaches is still a major challenge. Herein, a colorimetric biosensor was designed for the ultrasensitive and rapid detection of Gram-positive bacteria. The biosensor exploited polydopamine and polyethyleneimine (PDA-PEI)-modified papers for separating bacteria and carbon dots (CDs) for selective colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria. Noble metal-free CDs can target Gram-positive bacteria by binding with peptidoglycan and possess peroxidase-like activity. Thus, they can avert the step of modifying recognition probes, facilitating biosensor fabrication, and reducing the cost. This biosensor can detect S. aureus as low as 1 cfu mL−1, L. monocytogenes as low as 5 cfu mL−1, and B. subtilis as low as 9 cfu mL−1 within 55 min. In addition, a portable device was constructed to enable convenient and on-site quantitative detection of Gram-positive bacteria. The feasibility of the biosensor was verified by detecting Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples with recoveries ranging from 91.2% to 110%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetry
Separation
Polydopamine
Bacteria
Carbon dots
Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of gram-positive bacteria capture and colorimetric detection
Principle of colorimetric detection of gram-positive bacteria
Analysis of eggshell and sausage samples
Fabrication of a portable device for quantitatively detecting gram-positive bacteria
Comparison of the biosensor with other nanomaterial-mediated paper-based biosensors for colorimetric detection of bacteria

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21675056 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the procedure of the biosensor for the detection of Gram-positive bacteria.
SEM image of PDA-PEI@paper (bar 100 nm) (A). Characterization of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes by DLS (B). FT-IR spectra of pristine paper (black line) and PDA-PEI@paper (red line) (C).

TEM images (bar 100 nm and 5 nm) (A), FT-IR spectrum (B), XPS spectrum (C), and fluorescence spectrum (D) of CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixed solutions of 4-AAP, TBHBA, and H2O2 in the presence of different concentrations of CDs (E). Linear relationship between A510 and the concentration of CDs (n = 3) (F).

OD600 value of S. aureus (106 cfu mL−1) before and after incubation with PDA-PEI@paper (A). Specificity investigation of CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria (n = 3) (B). Effect of different concentrations of peptidoglycan/lipopolysaccharide on the F450 of CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) (n = 3) (C). The concentration of each bacterium was 107 cfu mL−1. UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixed solutions of 4-AAP, TBHBA, and H2O2 in the presence of S. aureus (PDA-PEI@paper@bacteria@CDs, red line) and absence of S. aureus (Control, black line) (D).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixed solutions containing S. aureus ranging from 101 to 106 cfu mL−1 (A). Linear relationship between A510 and the logarithm concentration of S. aureus (B). Specificity investigation of the biosensor toward Gram-positive bacteria (n = 3) (C). The concentration of each bacterium was 104 cfu mL−1.
Recovery experiments of Gram-positive bacteria in real samples by the colorimetric biosensor.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive colorimetric biosensor for the detection of Gram-positive bacteria by integrating paper-based enrichment and carbon dot-based selective recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510631, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510631, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid screening of bacteria by low-cost and eco-friendly material-based approaches is still a major challenge. Herein, a colorimetric biosensor was designed for the ultrasensitive and rapid detection of Gram-positive bacteria. The biosensor exploited polydopamine and polyethyleneimine (PDA-PEI)-modified papers for separating bacteria and carbon dots (CDs) for selective colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria. Noble metal-free CDs can target Gram-positive bacteria by binding with peptidoglycan and possess peroxidase-like activity. Thus, they can avert the step of modifying recognition probes, facilitating biosensor fabrication, and reducing the cost. This biosensor can detect S. aureus as low as 1 cfu mL−1, L. monocytogenes as low as 5 cfu mL−1, and B. subtilis as low as 9 cfu mL−1 within 55 min. In addition, a portable device was constructed to enable convenient and on-site quantitative detection of Gram-positive bacteria. The feasibility of the biosensor was verified by detecting Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples with recoveries ranging from 91.2% to 110%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Infections caused by Gram-positive bacteria can lead to many serious diseases, including sepsis, bacteremia, pneumonia, osteomyelitis, and endocarditis [1,2]. Rapid screening of Gram-positive bacteria-contaminated foodstuffs is the key to preventing and controlling the outbreaks of diseases induced by them [3]. As a gold standard method, the conventional plate colony counting approach requires a lengthy culture time, typically more than 24 h [4]. Thus, nucleic acid-, immunological-, and biosensor-based techniques have been developed for detecting bacteria [5–8]. They can efficiently reduce the detection time since they do not require cultural enrichment. Nevertheless, without cultural enrichment, the potential interference of a complex matrix on these methods may produce false-positive or negative results. Hence, developing preprocessing strategies to overcome matrices-associated inhibition and to concentrate low amounts of bacteria in foodstuffs is essential to ensure the accuracy of these methods.

Among the rapid detection techniques, colorimetric biosensors have attracted much attention due to their advantages, including low cost, simplicity, and practicality [9]. More importantly, the target-induced color changes can be easily read out by the naked eye, and therefore, expensive or sophisticated instrumentation is nonessential for colorimetric detection. In contrast, nucleic acid-based techniques rely on special instruments to accomplish the time-consuming polymerase chain reaction (PCR). Additionally, they must take time to extract nucleic acids as the template [10]. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA)-based techniques depend on MicroplateReader instruments to read and analyze the results. Additionally, they need time to prepare antibodies [11].

In colorimetric biosensors, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been utilized for bacterial detection according to the transformation of AuNP aggregation states triggered by bacteria [12]. Wu et al. designed a biosensor based on aptamer-functionalized AuNPs for detecting Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Salmonella typhimurium [13]. The detection process does not require specialized instrumentation or pretreatment steps. However, introducing aptamers will increase the detection cost, and the detection limit (105 cfu mL−1) is relatively high. More importantly, these biosensors are very susceptible to the sample matrix because the complex food matrices are liable to impact the aggregation states of functionalized AuNPs. Another strategy for designing colorimetric biosensors for bacterial detection is based on the color change produced by an enzyme-catalyzed substrate reaction [14]. Gold nanoclusters have been employed as nanoenzymes for fabricating colorimetric platforms for bacterial detection [15]. However, the complicated matrices in foodstuffs are fragile and affect the activity of nanoenzymes, thus influencing the accuracy of the results.

Solid phase extraction is an excellent strategy to avert matrix-associated interference [16]. Immunomagnetic separation utilizes antibody-modified magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to capture and enrich target bacteria [17,18]. However, introducing antibodies is costly, and MNPs are hard to degrade after use [19]. Paper is eco-friendly because it can be easily disposed of by incineration, and paper has drawn much attention due to its abundance, low cost, and simplicity [20]. Moreover, paper has been widely constructed as a functional material through surface modification [21]. Functionalized papers have been used for the pretreatment of organic additives and biological samples with high enrichment factors [22,23]. Dopamine, a central component of adhesive proteins in mussels, can form polydopamine (PDA) coatings on various material surfaces by oxidative self-polymerization in an alkaline aqueous medium [24]. The PDA coating can improve surface hydrophilicity and present excellent biocompatibility [25,26]. Our group discovered that PDA-modified MNPs can efficiently capture and concentrate bacteria [27].

Inspired by the above, we developed a colorimetric biosensor utilizing PDA and polyethyleneimine (PDA-PEI)-modified filter paper (PDA-PEI@paper) for the separation and concentration of bacteria and peptidoglycan-targeting carbon quantum dots (CDs) for the selective colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria. Noble metal-free CDs can target Gram-positive bacteria by binding with peptidoglycan and possessing peroxidase-mimicking activity. Therefore, CDs serve a dual purpose, the recognition probe and the nanozyme. CDs can avert the step of modifying recognition probes, such as antibodies or aptamers, facilitating the fabrication of the biosensor and reducing the cost. Furthermore, a laboratory-built portable device was constructed, which enables convenient and on-site quantitative detection of Gram-positive bacteria, further reducing the cost of the biosensor. The successful determination of Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples verified the feasibility of the biosensor.

The reagents and apparatus, bacteria culture, procedure for detection of Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples, and the method for construction of the portable device are given in the Supplementary Material.

PDA-PEI@paper was prepared according to a previously reported method [28] with some modifications. First, filter papers were pretreated with methanol three times to remove the impurity substance, followed by drying in a N2 environment at 50 °C. Then, the reaction solution was prepared by dissolving dopamine hydrochloride (2 mg mL−1) in 10 mM Tris-HCl solution (pH 8.5) containing PEI (0.1 mg mL−1). The pretreated papers were directly soaked in the reaction solution and incubated at room temperature for 24 h. Subsequently, the PDA-PEI@paper was washed with water and dried under a N2 atmosphere at 50 °C for 4 h.

CDs were synthesized by a previously reported approach [29] with minor modifications. In brief, d-glucose (0.6 mg mL−1), glycine (0.2 mg mL−1), and l-tryptophan (0.2 mg mL−1) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The formed CDs were collected by centrifugation at 12 000 rpm for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of gram-positive bacteria capture and colorimetric detection
ce:section.ce:para: The detection procedure of Gram-positive bacteria is schematically displayed in Fig. 1 . Typically, PDA-PEI@paper was added to centrifuge tubes containing various concentrations of Gram-positive bacteria (200 μL). After that, the centrifuge tubes were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min with shaking at 250 rpm. Subsequently, the CD solution (10 μL, 2.8 mg mL−1) was added to the mixture, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for another 20 min with shaking at 250 rpm. Then, the papers were clamped out by tweezers, soaked in deionized water, and the process was repeated three times to wash away the impurities.

For colorimetric detection, the bacteria-enriched papers were soaked in a mixed solution containing 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AAP) (60 μL, 10 mM, pH 8.5) and 2,4,6-tribromo-3-hydroxybenzoic acid (TBHBA) (800 μL, 10 mM, pH 8.5). Afterward, H2O2 (50 μL, 10 mM) was added to the mixed solution, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 5 min in the dark with shaking at 250 rpm. Then, the absorbance at 510 nm was recorded. All experiments were repeated three times.

The PDA-PEI@paper was prepared based on the oxidative self-polymerization of dopamine in a weakly alkaline solution (pH 8.5) and the reaction of PEI and oxidative dopamine [28]. Introducing PEI into the coating can enhance the amount of amino moiety, which is conducive to the adsorption of bacteria. In addition, PEI can prevent the aggregation of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes and uniformly anchor them on filter paper. As shown in the SEM image (Fig. 2 A), the nanocomplexes with an average diameter of approximately 50 nm were uniformly distributed on the surface of the filter papers. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement demonstrates that the hydrodynamic diameter of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes is 59.3 ± 9.6 nm (Fig. 2B), which is larger than that by SEM owing to the solvation of PDA-PEI.

The FT-IR analysis further verified the existence of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes on the surface of the filter papers. The spectrum of pristine filter papers (Fig. 2C, black line) displays an intense and broad band between 3600 and 3000 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of O–H groups. The peak at 2899 cm−1 results from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the C–H bond in the CH2OH groups. The peaks between 1400 and 900 cm−1 are attributed to the deformation, bending, or stretching vibrations of C–H and C–O bonds. After the filter paper was functionalized by PDA-PEI nanocomplexes (Fig. 2C, red line), the broad band shifted from 3330 to 3425 cm−1, demonstrating the presence of N–H bonds owing to the deposition of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes. Furthermore, a peak at 1628 cm−1 is attributed to the benzyl functional groups in PDA-PEI [4,30]. These results confirm that the filter paper was successfully functionalized with PDA-PEI nanocomplexes.

In addition, XRD analysis was used to characterize the PDA-PEI@paper. The XRD pattern of pristine filter paper (Fig. S2) shows the characteristic peaks at 2θ = 14.7°, 16.8°, and 22.8° corresponding to the (1
1 -
0), (110), and (220) crystallographic planes of monolithic cellulose type I, respectively [31]. The XRD pattern of the PDA-PEI@paper is similar to that of the pristine filter paper, suggesting that modification of PDA-PEI nanocomplexes does not damage the pristine filter paper.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by TEM. The TEM images (Fig. 3 A) demonstrate that the CDs are well dispersed with an average diameter of approximately 5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 3A) displays well-resolved lattice fringes of CDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon [32]. The FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3B) shows that CDs possess N–H and O–H groups at 3423 and 3180 cm−1. The peaks at 1628, 1450, and 1398 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–N and the symmetric stretching vibration of –COO, respectively [33,34]. The XPS spectrum of CDs exhibits three typical peaks at 285.6, 401.1, and 531.9 eV, which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [29] (Fig. 3C). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3) confirms the existence of CC/C–C (284.6 eV), C–O/C–N (285.6 eV), and –COOH (288.7 eV). The peaks of CO (531.7 eV) and C–O (532.9 eV) appear in the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3). C–N–C (399.7 eV) and N–H (401.5 eV) peaks emerge in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3). Consequently, the main functional groups on the surface of CDs are hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups. The absorbance spectrum of CDs (Fig. S4) exhibits three absorption peaks at 250, 315, and 375 nm. The peak at 250 nm is perhaps assigned to the π−π* transition of CC in the sp2 core [35]. The absorption peaks of CDs at 315 and 375 nm are designated as π−π* transitions of conjugated CC and CO [36]. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3D) demonstrates that the maximum fluorescence intensity at 450 nm can be achieved upon excitation at 375 nm.

To our surprise, CDs possess peroxidase-mimicking activity. The CDs can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). In the presence of ROS and CDs, TBHBA was activated and produced a free phenoxy radical [37]. A pinkish-purple quinone-imine dye product was generated through the reaction of the phenoxy radicals with 4-AAP and H2O2 (Fig. S5). As depicted in Fig. 3E, the absorbance at 510 nm (A510) increased with increasing CD concentration ranging from 0 to 15.5 μg mL−1, suggesting that CDs can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates. As illustrated in Fig. 3F, the A510 value demonstrates a linear response to the CD concentration with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.991.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of colorimetric detection of gram-positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus was employed as a Gram-positive bacterium model to explore the colorimetric biosensor principle. The biosensor was designed based on the separation and enrichment of PDA-PEI@paper for bacteria and CDs for the selective colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria, as shown in Fig. 1. The bacteria in the samples were first captured and concentrated by PDA-PEI@paper. Then, the enriched Gram-positive bacteria were selectively adsorbed by CDs. Next, the complex (PDA-PEI@paper@bacteria@CDs) can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates based on the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs, giving rise to the color change of the solution from colorless to pinkish-purple. The solution exhibits an absorbance peak at 510 nm. The more bacteria there are, the more CDs are adsorbed, and the color of the solution is darker. Thus, the concentration of Gram-positive bacteria can be quantified based on the absorbance of the solution at 510 nm. Several experiments were designed to confirm this principle.

First, an experiment was conducted to verify the enrichment of PDA-PEI@paper for bacteria. The OD600 of 106 cfu mL−1 of S. aureus was first recorded. Then, 200 μL of S. aureus (106 cfu mL−1) was incubated with PDA-PEI@paper in a shaker for 30 min, and the OD600 of the solution was then recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the OD600 of the solution after incubation with PDA-PEI@paper significantly declined, indicating that the bacteria were successfully captured by PDA-PEI@paper based on the hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions between the amino and hydroxy groups on the PDA-PEI coating and the functional moieties possessed by bacteria [27].

Second, the selective capture of Gram-positive bacteria by CDs was verified. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence of the CD solution remained unchanged after centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min. Then, six strains of bacteria (107 cfu mL−1) were incubated with CDs (3.1 μg mL−1) for 20 min, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (F450) of each supernatant was detected. As shown in Fig. 4B, the F450 values of the supernatants in the presence of Gram-positive bacteria were significantly lower than those of Gram-negative bacteria, suggesting the selectivity of CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria. Moreover, the selectivity of CDs to Gram-positive bacteria was demonstrated experimentally. The cell wall composition of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria is different. Gram-positive bacteria contain more peptidoglycan layers in the cell wall than Gram-negative bacteria. The outermost layer of the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria consists of lipopolysaccharide [38]. Thus, the effects of peptidoglycan and lipopolysaccharide on the fluorescence of CDs were investigated. Varying amounts (0−1.5 mg mL−1) of peptidoglycan or lipopolysaccharide were mixed with CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) for 5 min in the dark with shaking at 250 rpm. The fluorescence intensity of the samples was measured. As shown in Fig. 4C, peptidoglycan triggered a significant decrease in the F450 of CDs. In contrast, the change in F450 induced by lipopolysaccharide is less than 12%. The result demonstrates that the selectivity of CDs to Gram-positive bacteria is ascribed to CDs targeting the peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria.

Third, experiments were conducted to verify whether the bacteria captured by PDA-PEI@paper can adsorb CDs. Fig. 4D demonstrates that the presence of S. aureus gives rise to a significant absorbance at 510 nm compared to the control. The result suggests the feasibility of the biosensor for detecting Gram-positive bacteria. Notably, a negligible increase in A510 in the control group can be observed because the cellulosic paper is a porous structure substrate, and a trace amount of CDs was unavoidably trapped.

The paper-based separation of bacteria from samples plays a vital role in ensuring the accuracy and sensitivity of a colorimetric biosensor. The surface area of PDA-PEI@paper and the sample volume significantly impact the enrichment efficiency of the paper for bacteria. Thus, these factors were first optimized.

First, the shape of the PDA-PEI@paper was customized in a teardrop shape to fit the bottom of the centrifuge tube, as displayed in Fig. S7. The volume of the bacterial solution was optimized by fixing the surface area of PDA-PEI@paper. As shown in Fig. S8, the paper can be fully submerged in a 200 μL sample solution. If the sample solution is less than 200 μL, the paper cannot be fully immersed. Beyond 200 μL, the enrichment efficiency will be decreased. The results demonstrate that 200 μL of sample solution can maximize enrichment efficiency.

After that, the adsorption capacity of PDA-PEI@paper for bacteria was measured. Initially, the linear relationship between the OD600 of S. aureus and the colony-forming unit (cfu) of bacteria was measured through a plate counting method. The linear equation is OD600 = 3.548 × 10−8C +0.048 (R2 = 0.999) in the range between 1.0 × 106 cfu mL−1 and 3.0 × 107 cfu mL−1 (Fig. S9). Then, the PDA-PEI@papers were incubated with different concentrations of bacterial solutions (5.0 × 106 cfu mL−1 to 3.0 × 107 cfu mL−1, 200 μL) for 30 min, and the concentration of residual bacteria in each supernatant was measured. The adsorbed amount of bacteria was calculated by subtracting the residual amount from the initial amount. As shown in Fig. S10, the adsorption capacity of PDA-PEI@paper for S. aureus was approximately 7 × 105 cfu per piece.

Subsequently, S. aureus (105 cfu mL−1) was used as a model bacterium to optimize the detection conditions of the biosensor for Gram-positive bacteria. A510 was used as a metric to optimize the experimental conditions. The parameters, including incubation temperature (A), pH of sample solution (10 mM phosphate, B), capture time for bacteria (C), and adsorption time for CDs (D), were optimized by an orthogonal test. First, single-factor experiments were carried out to screen out the appropriate ranges of these factors. Then, orthogonal tests were designed and analyzed using Minitab software (version 19) ([L25 (54)]). As illustrated in Fig. S11, the best detection conditions were the capture of bacteria (30 min) and adsorption of CDs (20 min) carried out in phosphate solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) at 37 °C.

Quantitative analysis was carried out by measuring the A510 of the pinkish-purple products in solutions containing different concentrations of Gram-positive bacteria, and the results are listed in Table S1. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, A510 increased with increasing logarithm concentration of S. aureus. The increase in A510 showed a fine linear relationship with the logarithm concentration of S. aureus from 101 to 106 cfu mL−1 (R2 of 0.989) (Fig. 5B). Moreover, as shown in Figs. S12–S13, the biosensor also exhibits a fine linear correlation versus the logarithm concentrations of the other two Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes and B. subtilis) (R2 ≥ 0.933), demonstrating the feasibility of this biosensor in detecting Gram-positive bacteria. The LODs based on 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio were computed to be 1 cfu mL−1 for S. aureus, 5 cfu mL−1 for L. monocytogenes, and 9 cfu mL−1 for B. subtilis, respectively.

The specificity of the colorimetric biosensor was evaluated. Under optimum conditions, four strains of Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli O157:H7 [E. coli], Pseudomonas aeruginosa [P. aeruginosa], Proteus vulgaris [P. vulgaris], and Enterobacter sakazakii [E. sakazakii], each concentration 104 cfu mL−1) were detected. As depicted in Fig. 5C, the absorbance of the detection system at 510 nm in the presence of Gram-positive bacteria was more than twofold more prominent than that of Gram-negative bacteria since CDs can target Gram-positive bacteria by binding with peptidoglycan (Fig. 4B). The results indicate that the colorimetric biosensor has good specificity and selectivity for Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of eggshell and sausage samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the biosensor in actual samples was validated by analyzing eggshell and sausage samples. Blank eggshell and sausage samples showed no bacteria using the plate counting method. Therefore, eggshell and sausage samples spiked with three Gram-positive bacteria at various concentrations were analyzed. As shown in Figs. S14–S19, A510 has a good linear relationship with the logarithm of Gram-positive bacteria concentration in the samples ranging from 101 to 106 cfu mL−1 (R2 ≥ 0.903). As shown in Table 1 , two concentrations of each Gram-positive bacterium were added to the two samples for recovery experiments. The recoveries varied between 91.2% and 103%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 5.6%. These results demonstrate that the colorimetric biosensor is an ideal substitute for differentiating and detecting Gram-positive bacteria in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of a portable device for quantitatively detecting gram-positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S1, a laboratory-built portable device was designed and fabricated by a 3D printer to realize the convenient and on-site detection of bacteria by the biosensor. The quantitative results of three stains of Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples by the portable device are shown in Figs. S20–S25. A510 linearly responds to the logarithm concentration of the spiked Gram-positive bacteria (R2 ≥ 0.960). The linear equations agree with those achieved by a UV–vis spectrometer. As shown in Table S2, two concentrations of each Gram-positive bacterium were added to the two samples for recovery experiments. The recoveries ranged between 93.2% and 110%, with RSDs less than 5.8%. Video S1 shows the procedure for detecting samples by the portable device. The results indicate that the portable device can realize the on-site quantitative detection of Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the biosensor with other nanomaterial-mediated paper-based biosensors for colorimetric detection of bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensor was compared with previously reported nanomaterial-mediated paper-based biosensors for the colorimetric detection of bacteria [39–42]. As shown in Table S3, the biosensor demonstrates absolute superiority in terms of LOD and detection range over other sensors. Other sensors are mediated with noble metal nanoparticles to achieve colorimetric detection and functionalized with antibodies or aptamers to realize selectivity toward the target bacteria. Introducing noble metals and antibodies/aptamers undoubtedly raises the cost of biosensors. In addition, the biosensor exploits a portable optical device to quantitatively measure bacteria, further reducing the detection cost.

We developed a colorimetric biosensor using PDA-PEI@paper for the separation and concentration of target bacteria and CDs for selective colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria. The PDA-PEI@paper-based separation method can avert sample matrix-associated interference and enrich bacteria, thus improving the detection sensitivity. The LODs were as low as 1 cfu mL−1, L. monocytogenes as low as 5 cfu mL−1, and B. subtilis as low as 9 cfu mL−1, demonstrating absolute superiority over other nanomaterial-mediated paper-based biosensors. CDs can specifically recognize Gram-positive bacteria by selectively targeting the peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria. Hence, the biosensor avoids introducing additional recognition elements, facilitating the fabrication of the biosensor and reducing the cost. In addition, the biosensor is environmentally friendly since it is composed of biocompatible material-modified paper and carbon dots. Fabricating a laboratory-built portable optical detector further decreases the detection cost. The successful detection of Gram-positive bacteria in eggshell and sausage samples with a complex matrix verified the feasibility of the biosensor in actual samples. The results suggest that integrating paper-based solid phase extraction with colorimetric detection has great potential in food quantity control.

Li Fu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Suqi Deng: Validation. Yimin Luo: Validation. Qiang Fu: Validation. Yi Fan: Validation. Li Jia: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675056).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124920.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dendrimer-based multivalent vancomycin nanoplatform for rargeting the drug-resistant bacterial surface

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Choi, A. Myc, J.E. Silpe, M. Sumit, P.T. Wong, K. McCarthy, A.M. Desai, T.P. Thomas, A. Kotlyar, M.M.B. Holl, B.G. Orr, J.R. Baker, Jr., Dendrimer-based multivalent vancomycin nanoplatform for rargeting the drug-resistant bacterial surface, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 214-228. https://doi.org/10.1021/nn3038995.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ubiquitous detection of Gram-positive bacteria with bioorthogonal magnetofluorescent nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a rapid and sensitive immunosensor for the detection of bacteria
